    bedrooms_c, recreation, lotsize_1000_c_sq, bed_bath_c, bath_story_c, garage_prefer_c

  • The idea: The first line is “what actually matters” for explaining price in the final model. The second line is “extra stuff” that did not improve prediction enough to keep.

Code 
kable(house.summary, row.names = FALSE)
Metric Value
Selection Used Post-Cook AIC/CV agreement
Adjusted R2 0.7434
Residual s 11277.06
Outliers Removed 34
Test RMSE 15561.44
Test R2 0.6491
Code 
# Display a compact summary of how well the final model performs.
# The table shows:
#   - Adjusted R2: how much of the variation in prices the model explains,
#                  adjusted for how many predictors we use.
#   - Residual s: the typical size of the errors on the training data,
#                 in the same units as price (lower is better).
#   - Outliers Removed: how many unusually influential sales we removed
#                       (via Cook filtering) before fitting this final model.
#   - Test RMSE: average prediction error on the 20% test set that the model
#                never saw during training (lower is better).
#   - Test R2: how much variation in prices the model explains on that test set.
# Together, these numbers give a high-level view of model fit and
# how well it predicts new house prices.

class: tiny-table compact small-title

HousePrices: Final Model Terms

Code 
# Coefficient table for the final HousePrices model, sorted by p-value.
kable(house.key, row.names = FALSE)
Term Estimate P Value
stories_c 7805.6955 < 1e-16
bathrooms_c 12852.9679 < 1e-16
airconyes 9729.5636 7.14e-12
preferyes 9546.3443 2.94e-10
fullbaseyes 6006.3710 3.34e-06
log_lotsize_c 29548.4905 8.23e-06
gasheatyes 13901.8788 4.30e-05
garage_c 2565.4854 0.000541
drivewayyes 4261.8654 0.013657
garage_c:lotsize_1000_c 874.8578 0.022088
lotsize_c -2.3979 0.046558
preferyes:log_lotsize_c 5978.7046 0.071843
airconyes:log_lotsize_c 6490.6674 0.086865
  • Larger lots, more stories and bathrooms, air conditioning, a garage, and preferred location all move fitted price upward when the other features are held fixed.
  • The coefficient table shows that the strongest evidence sits in structural, amenity, and retained engineered terms rather than in one single dominant feature.

class: tiny-table compact small-title

HousePrices: Engineered Terms Kept

Code 
# Show every engineered HousePrices candidate that entered the expanded full
# model, then mark whether the post-Cook final model kept it.
house.engineered.pool <- c(house.derived.terms, house.interaction.terms)
house.final.terms <- get_model_terms(house.model.final)
house.selected.derived <- intersect(house.derived.terms, house.final.terms)
house.selected.interactions <- intersect(house.interaction.terms, house.final.terms)

house.engineered.status <- data.frame(
  Term = house.engineered.pool,
  Type = c(
    rep("Derived", length(house.derived.terms)),
    rep("Interaction", length(house.interaction.terms))
  ),
  Meaning = c(
    "Centered natural log of lot size.",
    "Squared centered lot size in thousands.",
    "Centered bedrooms multiplied by centered bathrooms.",
    "Centered bathrooms multiplied by centered stories.",
    "Centered garage spaces multiplied by preferred-neighborhood indicator.",
    "Centered log lot size slope allowed to differ by preferred-neighborhood status.",
    "Centered log lot size slope allowed to differ by air-conditioning status.",
    "Centered garage-space effect allowed to vary by centered lot size."
  ),
  `Kept In Final Model` = ifelse(house.engineered.pool %in% house.final.terms, "Yes", "No"),
  check.names = FALSE
)

kable(house.engineered.status, row.names = FALSE)
Term Type Meaning Kept In Final Model
log_lotsize_c Derived Centered natural log of lot size. Yes
lotsize_1000_c_sq Derived Squared centered lot size in thousands. No
bed_bath_c Interaction Centered bedrooms multiplied by centered bathrooms. No
bath_story_c Interaction Centered bathrooms multiplied by centered stories. No
garage_prefer_c Interaction Centered garage spaces multiplied by preferred-neighborhood indicator. No
log_lotsize_c:prefer Interaction Centered log lot size slope allowed to differ by preferred-neighborhood status. No
log_lotsize_c:aircon Interaction Centered log lot size slope allowed to differ by air-conditioning status. No
garage_c:lotsize_1000_c Interaction Centered garage-space effect allowed to vary by centered lot size. Yes
  • AIC/CV considered these terms from the start; they were not pasted on after the final model was chosen.
  • Retained derived terms mean the house-price relationship is not purely linear in the original variables.
  • Retained interactions mean at least one housing feature changes slope depending on another feature or amenity.

class: plot-slide small-table

HousePrices: Diagnostics

Code 
# Visual residual checks: normality with QQ plot and equal-variance pattern
# with residuals vs fitted values.
par(mfrow = c(1, 2))
qqnorm(house.model.final$residuals, main = "QQ Plot")
qqline(house.model.final$residuals)
plot(
  house.model.final$fitted.values,
  house.model.final$residuals,
  pch = 16,
  xlab = "Fitted",
  ylab = "Residuals",
  main = "Residuals vs Fitted"
)
abline(h = 0, lty = "dashed")
Code 
par(mfrow = c(1, 1))
Code 
# Formal diagnostic p-values corresponding to the residual-check slide.
kable(house.diag, row.names = FALSE)
Test P.Value
Shapiro-Wilk 0.0035
Breusch-Pagan 0.0353
Durbin-Watson 5.95e-07
  • The residual checks do not show a single breakdown large enough to undermine the model for the main inference and prediction goals here.

class: small-table plot-slide compact

HousePrices: Box-Cox Response Check

Code 
# Box-Cox profiles possible response transformations. Lambda = 1 corresponds
# to no transformation, so its location relative to the 95% cutoff determines
# whether the profile plot gives transformation evidence.
house.boxcox <- boxcox_fit_and_evaluate(
  house.model.final,
  house.train.new,
  house.test,
  "price",
  "HousePrices"
)

plot_boxcox_profile(house.boxcox$profile, "HousePrices Box-Cox profile")
Code 
# Compare on the original price scale so the transformed-response model is not
# rewarded just for changing units.
kable(boxcox_decision_table(
  house.boxcox,
  ifelse(
    house.boxcox$profile$identity.in.interval,
    "Keep original price scale; lambda = 1 remains plausible.",
    "Report as sensitivity evidence; lambda = 1 is outside the profile interval."
  )
), row.names = FALSE)
Best Lambda Approx. 95% Lambda Interval Lambda 1 In Interval Decision
0.52 [0.33, 0.7] FALSE Report as sensitivity evidence; lambda = 1 is outside the profile interval.
  • The profile peaks near lambda 0.52; lambda = 1 is outside the approximate 95% interval.
  • We keep the original-scale model as the main result when interpretability and held-out performance are comparable; Box-Cox is the assumption sensitivity check.

class: small-table compact

HousePrices: Box-Cox Test Metrics

Code 
# These rows compare final-model predictions on the held-out test set after
# converting any Box-Cox prediction back to dollar scale.
kable(boxcox_performance_table(house.boxcox), row.names = FALSE)
Dataset Model Lambda Test R2 Test RMSE Shapiro P BP P
HousePrices Current final NA 0.6491 15561.44 0.0035 0.0353
HousePrices Box-Cox naive back-transform 0.52 0.6532 15470.60 0.5007 0.5137
HousePrices Box-Cox smearing back-transform 0.52 0.6522 15493.34 0.5007 0.5137
  • The comparison is made on the original price scale, so RMSE remains interpretable as average dollar prediction error.
  • The smearing version corrects the back-transform for residual spread on the transformed scale.
  • We treat Box-Cox as a sensitivity model unless it clearly improves held-out RMSE and keeps interpretation acceptable.

class: plot-slide

HousePrices: Bootstrap Coefficient Intervals

Code 
# Prepare the bootstrap coefficient interval plot. The intercept is removed
# because it is on a much larger scale than the predictor coefficients.
house.boot.plot <- subset(house.boot, term != "(Intercept)")
house.boot.plot <- house.boot.plot[nrow(house.boot.plot):1, ]

plot(
  house.boot.plot$estimate,
  seq_len(nrow(house.boot.plot)),
  xlim = range(c(house.boot.plot$lower, house.boot.plot$upper)),
  pch = 16,
  yaxt = "n",
  ylab = "",
  xlab = "Coefficient estimate",
  main = "Bootstrap 95% intervals for final-model coefficients"
)

segments(
  house.boot.plot$lower, seq_len(nrow(house.boot.plot)),
  house.boot.plot$upper, seq_len(nrow(house.boot.plot)),
  lwd = 2,
  col = "#0b6e4f"
)

abline(v = 0, lty = "dashed", col = "gray50")
axis(2, at = seq_len(nrow(house.boot.plot)), labels = house.boot.plot$term, las = 2)
  • These are empirical 95% intervals from 200 bootstrap resamples of the final training set.
  • Intervals far from 0 support stable coefficient direction.
  • The intercept is omitted because its much larger scale would compress the predictor effects.
  • The most practically important retained terms stay on one side of 0, which supports stable sign interpretation.

class: small-table

HousePrices: Prediction Examples

Code 
# Show fitted values, confidence intervals, and prediction intervals for the
# two manually defined house examples.
kable(house.pred.table, row.names = FALSE)
Scenario Fit CI PI
Smaller home 49203 (46814, 51593) (26905, 71502)
Larger home 130632 (122694, 138570) (107084, 154180)
  • The confidence interval estimates the mean price.
  • The prediction interval is wider because it predicts an individual home sale.
  • The higher-amenity scenario also gets the higher fitted price, which matches the coefficient signs in the final model.

class: plot-slide

HousePrices: CI and PI Overlay

Code 
# Overlay fitted means, CIs, and PIs on the actual cleaned training data.
# New prediction examples are plotted at their fitted x-location.
plot(
  house.fit.x,
  house.train.new$price[house.order],
  pch = 16,
  col = "gray40",
  xlab = "Fitted mean response (ordered low to high)",
  ylab = "Price",
  main = "Actual data with fitted CI/PI and new prediction intervals"
)

lines(house.fit.x, house.fit.x, col = "#0b6e4f", lwd = 2)
lines(house.fit.x, house.actual.conf[house.order, "lwr"], col = "#0b6e4f", lty = 2)
lines(house.fit.x, house.actual.conf[house.order, "upr"], col = "#0b6e4f", lty = 2)
lines(house.fit.x, house.actual.pred[house.order, "lwr"], col = "#c97c10", lty = 3)
lines(house.fit.x, house.actual.pred[house.order, "upr"], col = "#c97c10", lty = 3)

points(house.new.fit, house.new.fit, pch = 17, col = "#b83227", cex = 1.3)
segments(house.new.fit, house.pred[, "lwr"], house.new.fit, house.pred[, "upr"],
         col = "#f0b44c", lwd = 2)
segments(house.new.fit, house.conf[, "lwr"], house.new.fit, house.conf[, "upr"],
         col = "#b83227", lwd = 3)
  • Gray points are actual observed prices.
  • The x-axis is fitted mean response, sorted from low to high.
  • The green line is the fitted mean.
  • Orange bands show PI for observed-data rows.
  • Red triangles and bars now sit at their fitted locations on the green line.
  • The wider orange bands show that individual-home prediction is much harder than estimating the mean response.

class: plot-slide

HousePrices: Predicted vs Actual (Test Set)

Code 
# Final visual check on the held-out test set: points near the 45-degree line
# are accurate predictions.
plot(
  house.prediction,
  house.test$price,
  pch = 16,
  col = adjustcolor("#2a6f97", alpha.f = 0.6),
  xlab = "Predicted Price",
  ylab = "Actual Price",
  main = "Predicted vs Actual — held-out test set"
)
abline(0, 1, col = "#b83227", lwd = 2, lty = 2)
legend(
  "topleft",
  legend = "Perfect prediction line (slope = 1)",
  col = "#b83227",
  lty = 2,
  lwd = 2,
  bty = "n"
)
  • Points close to the dashed line indicate accurate test-set predictions.
  • Deviations are largest at high price values, showing more uncertainty for expensive homes.
  • The overall scatter is consistent with the test RMSE reported in the final model summary.

CASchools: Dataset Snapshot

.pull-left[ - 420 observations and 9 original analysis variables - Response: average test score score - str: students per teacher - calworks: percent of students on CalWORKs - lunch: percent eligible for subsidized lunch - income: district income level - english: percent of English learners - grades: district grade-span category; one fitted indicator is gradesKK-08 for K-8 districts - Missing values: 0 - Centered variables, squared terms, and socioeconomic interactions are created after the train/test split. - The covariates mix school resources with student-composition measures, so the model is mainly explanatory rather than causal.]

.pull-right[

Code 
# Simple dataset-audit table for the transformed CASchools analysis data.
kable(
  data.frame(
    Item = c("Rows", "Columns after engineering", "Missing"),
    Value = c(nrow(schools.data), ncol(schools.data), sum(is.na(schools.data)))
  ),
  row.names = FALSE
)
Item Value
Rows 420
Columns after engineering 22
Missing 0

]

class: plot-slide

CASchools: Exploratory Overview

Code 
# EDA focuses on the response and the two strongest socioeconomic patterns:
# subsidized lunch share and district income.
par(mfrow = c(1, 3), mar = c(4.5, 4, 3, 1))

hist(
  schools.data$score,
  breaks = 25,
  col = "#2a6f97",
  border = "white",
  xlab = "Score",
  main = "Distribution of Score"
)

plot(
  schools.data$lunch,
  schools.data$score,
  pch = 16,
  col = adjustcolor("#0b6e4f", alpha.f = 0.5),
  xlab = "% Subsidized Lunch",
  ylab = "Score",
  main = "Score vs Lunch"
)

plot(
  schools.data$income,
  schools.data$score,
  pch = 16,
  col = adjustcolor("#b83227", alpha.f = 0.5),
  xlab = "District Income",
  ylab = "Score",
  main = "Score vs Income"
)
Code 
par(mfrow = c(1, 1), mar = c(5.1, 4.1, 4.1, 2.1))
  • Score is roughly symmetric and bell-shaped across districts.
  • The negative relationship with lunch rate is the strongest raw signal in the data.
  • Income shows a clear positive association, confirming that socioeconomic factors dominate the score pattern.

class: small-table

CASchools: Interim Process

Code 
# Print the CASchools workflow table created in the setup chunk.
kable(schools.process, row.names = FALSE)
Step Detail
Split data 324 train / 96 test
Create centered engineered terms 10 derived/interaction candidates
Diagnose collinearity 0 high-correlation pairs flagged; 0 pre-AIC removals
Build centered expanded full model 18 centered starting predictors
Build model ladder 18 nested steps
Run repeated CV 5 folds x 6 repeats
Compare AIC and CV Step 12 vs Step 12
Remove outliers 19 points above Cook cutoff
Rerun diagnostics and AIC/CV after Cook filtering 0 post-Cook high-correlation pairs; Step 12 AIC and Step 12 CV
Check residuals QQ, BP, and DW tests
Check Box-Cox transformation profile plot and original-scale test metrics
Bootstrap intervals 200 bootstrap resamples
Predict districts 2 cases with CI and PI
  • The main question is whether school-score variation is driven more by student composition than by resource variables.
  • Engineered socioeconomic terms are included before AIC/CV, so nonlinear and interaction patterns must earn their place in the selected model.

class: small-table compact

CASchools: VIF and Correlation Diagnostics

Code 
# Correlation and VIF show redundancy risk in the expanded school-score
# candidate set. They are diagnostics only, not a pre-AIC filter.
kable(format_screen_summary(schools.collinearity, "Initial training data"), row.names = FALSE)
Stage High Correlation Pairs Max Adjusted VIF Terms Sent to AIC/CV Pre-AIC Removals
Initial training data 0 42.82 18 None; AIC/CV selects from full centered set
Code 
kable(format_screen_summary(schools.clean.collinearity, "Cook-filtered training data"), row.names = FALSE)
Stage High Correlation Pairs Max Adjusted VIF Terms Sent to AIC/CV Pre-AIC Removals
Cook-filtered training data 0 39.91 18 None; AIC/CV selects from full centered set
Code 
# Final VIFs are shown after Cook filtering because that is the training data
# used to build the reported final model.
kable(format_final_vif_table(schools.clean.collinearity, max_rows = 6), row.names = FALSE)
Term Adjusted VIF
lunch_c 39.91
english_c 37.53
english_c:grades 36.93
lunch_c:grades 35.03
income_c 19.85
income_c:grades 15.48
  • Higher VIFs are expected when socioeconomic variables, squares, and interactions describe overlapping district conditions.
  • We document the redundancy, then let AIC/CV decide whether any engineered term improves prediction enough to keep.
  • This avoids deleting interaction terms just because they are naturally correlated with the variables that created them.

class: plot-slide

CASchools: CV Across the Selection Ladder

Code 
# Same CV ladder idea as HousePrices: each box contains 30 validation RMSEs
# for one model step, and the line shows the average across those folds.
par(mar = c(7, 4.5, 4, 1))

boxplot(
  RMSE ~ factor(step_num, labels = schools.cv.labels),
  data = schools.cv,
  las = 2,
  cex.axis = 0.8,
  xlab = "",
  ylab = "Validation RMSE",
  main = "Repeated 5-fold CV on the training-set model ladder"
)

lines(seq_along(schools.cv.labels), schools.cv.summary$RMSE, col = "#123c55", lwd = 2)
points(seq_along(schools.cv.labels), schools.cv.summary$RMSE, pch = 16, col = "#123c55")
points(schools.cv.best.idx, schools.cv.summary$RMSE[schools.cv.best.idx], pch = 19, col = "#0b6e4f", cex = 1.2)

abline(h = schools.aic.cv.rmse, col = "#b83227", lty = 2, lwd = 2)
legend(
  "topleft",
  legend = c("Mean CV RMSE", "CV-best step", "AIC-selected step mean CV RMSE"),
  col = c("#123c55", "#0b6e4f", "#b83227"),
  pch = c(16, 19, NA),
  lty = c(1, NA, 2),
  lwd = c(2, NA, 2),
  bty = "n"
)
Code 
par(mar = c(5.1, 4.1, 4.1, 2.1))
  • The ladder is built from the training-set full model.
  • Each step again summarizes 30 validation RMSE values from 5 folds repeated 6 times.
  • This checks whether a simpler school-score model is competitive before touching the test set.
  • Here the AIC step and CV step agree, so the red line lands at the best step.
  • Agreement between AIC and CV makes the reduced school model much more defensible.

class: small-table

CASchools: AIC Step vs CV Step

Code 
# Table version of the AIC-vs-CV decision for CASchools.
kable(schools.compare.display, row.names = FALSE)
Model Best Step Predictors CV RMSE
AIC-optimal Step 12 6 8.444
CV-optimal Step 12 6 8.444
  • The agreement here says the AIC-selected reduction is not just an artifact of one criterion.

class: small-table

CASchools: Sensitivity to Cook Filtering

Code 
# Validation sensitivity table: compares repeated-CV RMSE before and after
# influential districts are removed from the training data.
kable(schools.sensitivity.display, row.names = FALSE)
Training Data Used AIC Best Step AIC RMSE CV Best Step CV RMSE
Original training data Step 12 8.44 Step 12 8.44
Cook-filtered training data Step 12 7.14 Step 12 7.14
  • The main question is whether influential districts materially change the selected CV step or the full validation curve.
Code 
# Held-out test table: final prediction check on the 20% test set.
kable(schools.test.display, row.names = FALSE)
Training Data Used AIC Best Step CV Best Step Held-out Test RMSE
Original training data Step 12 Step 12 8.39 (same for AIC and CV)
Cook-filtered training data Step 12 Step 12 8.40 (same for AIC and CV)
  • In the upper table, both RMSE columns are repeated-CV validation RMSE.
  • In the lower table, the RMSE is computed on the held-out 20% test set by comparing predictions to the actual observed response values.
  • When AIC and CV pick the same step, that test RMSE is shown once.
  • The selected step does not move after filtering, so the main school result is not being driven by a few extreme districts.

class: small-table plot-slide

CASchools: CV Ladder Before and After Cook Filtering

Code 
# Same before/after Cook-filtering plot structure used for HousePrices. The
# shared y-axis makes the validation-error change visually comparable.
par(mfrow = c(1, 3), mar = c(7, 4.2, 3.5, 1))

boxplot(
  RMSE ~ factor(step_num, labels = schools.cv.labels),
  data = schools.cv,
  las = 2,
  cex.axis = 0.8,
  xlab = "",
  ylab = "Validation RMSE",
  ylim = schools.cv.ylim,
  main = "Before Cook filtering"
)
lines(seq_len(nrow(schools.cv.summary)), schools.cv.summary$RMSE, col = "#123c55", lwd = 2)
points(seq_len(nrow(schools.cv.summary)), schools.cv.summary$RMSE, pch = 16, col = "#123c55")
abline(h = schools.aic.cv.rmse, col = "#b83227", lty = 2, lwd = 2)
legend(
  "topleft",
  legend = c("Mean CV RMSE", "AIC-selected step"),
  col = c("#123c55", "#b83227"),
  pch = c(16, NA),
  lty = c(1, 2),
  lwd = c(2, 2),
  bty = "n",
  cex = 0.8
)

plot(
  schools.cv.summary$step_num,
  schools.cv.summary$RMSE,
  type = "b",
  pch = 16,
  lwd = 2,
  col = "gray40",
  xaxt = "n",
  xlab = "",
  ylab = "Mean validation RMSE",
  ylim = schools.cv.ylim,
  main = "Mean CV RMSE overlay"
)
lines(
  schools.clean.cv.summary$step_num,
  schools.clean.cv.summary$RMSE,
  type = "b",
  pch = 17,
  lwd = 2,
  col = "#0b6e4f"
)
abline(h = schools.aic.cv.rmse, col = "gray40", lty = 2, lwd = 2)
abline(h = schools.clean.aic.cv.rmse, col = "#0b6e4f", lty = 2, lwd = 2)
axis(1, at = schools.cv.summary$step_num, labels = schools.cv.labels, las = 2, cex.axis = 0.8)
legend(
  "topleft",
  legend = c("Before Cook", "After Cook", "Before AIC RMSE", "After AIC RMSE"),
  col = c("gray40", "#0b6e4f", "gray40", "#0b6e4f"),
  pch = c(16, 17, NA, NA),
  lty = c(1, 1, 2, 2),
  lwd = c(2, 2, 2, 2),
  bty = "n",
  cex = 0.8
)

boxplot(
  RMSE ~ factor(step_num, labels = schools.clean.cv.labels),
  data = schools.clean.cv,
  las = 2,
  cex.axis = 0.8,
  xlab = "",
  ylab = "Validation RMSE",
  ylim = schools.cv.ylim,
  main = "After Cook filtering"
)
lines(seq_len(nrow(schools.clean.cv.summary)), schools.clean.cv.summary$RMSE, col = "#123c55", lwd = 2)
points(seq_len(nrow(schools.clean.cv.summary)), schools.clean.cv.summary$RMSE, pch = 16, col = "#123c55")
abline(h = schools.clean.aic.cv.rmse, col = "#b83227", lty = 2, lwd = 2)
legend(
  "topleft",
  legend = c("Mean CV RMSE", "AIC-selected step"),
  col = c("#123c55", "#b83227"),
  pch = c(16, NA),
  lty = c(1, 2),
  lwd = c(2, 2),
  bty = "n",
  cex = 0.8
)
Code 
par(mfrow = c(1, 1), mar = c(5.1, 4.1, 4.1, 2.1))
  • The best step stays the same after Cook filtering, but the full curve shifts down, which makes the selected model look more stable.
  • That means filtering helps fit quality, but it does not change which predictors matter.

class: tiny-table compact

CASchools: Final Model

  • Final predictors kept:

lunch_c, computer.rate_c, income_c, english_c, grades, low_income_pressure_c

  • Removed from full model:

str_c, calworks_c, expenditure_c, income_c_sq, lunch_c_sq, english_c_sq, income_lunch_c, str_lunch_c, lunch_c:grades, income_c:grades, english_c:grades, str_c:grades

.pull-left[

Code 
# Left table gives overall final-model fit and held-out test performance.
kable(schools.summary, row.names = FALSE)
Metric Value
Selection Used Post-Cook AIC/CV agreement
Adjusted R2 0.8485
Residual s 7.04
Outliers Removed 19
Test RMSE 8.4
Test R2 0.819

]

.pull-right[

Code 
# Right table gives final-model coefficient estimates and p-values.
kable(schools.key, row.names = FALSE)
Term Estimate P Value
lunch_c -0.3424 < 1e-16
income_c 0.7782 5.09e-15
english_c -0.3031 2.32e-11
gradesKK-08 -3.8886 0.00117
low_income_pressure_c 0.0029 0.00658
computer.rate_c 18.1300 0.00773

]

  • gradesKK-08 is kept in the pooled model because it captures a real district-type difference; dropping it would likely push that effect into the other coefficients.
  • The model-selection process also saw squared and interaction terms, so omitted engineered terms are evidence that their extra complexity did not improve the post-Cook ladder enough.
  • Once poverty proxies and district type are in the model, extra resource variables add much less explanatory power.

class: tiny-table compact small-title

CASchools: Engineered Terms Kept

Code 
# Show the engineered CASchools candidates and whether they survived the final
# post-Cook model-selection process.
schools.engineered.pool <- c(schools.derived.terms, schools.interaction.terms)
schools.final.terms <- get_model_terms(schools.model.final)
schools.selected.derived <- intersect(schools.derived.terms, schools.final.terms)
schools.selected.interactions <- intersect(schools.interaction.terms, schools.final.terms)

schools.engineered.status <- data.frame(
  Term = schools.engineered.pool,
  Type = c(
    rep("Derived", length(schools.derived.terms)),
    rep("Interaction", length(schools.interaction.terms))
  ),
  Meaning = c(
    "Squared centered district income.",
    "Squared centered subsidized-lunch percentage.",
    "Squared centered English-learner percentage.",
    "Centered lunch percentage multiplied by centered English-learner percentage.",
    "Centered income multiplied by centered lunch percentage.",
    "Centered student-teacher ratio multiplied by centered lunch percentage.",
    "Centered lunch slope allowed to differ by district grade span.",
    "Centered income slope allowed to differ by district grade span.",
    "Centered English-learner slope allowed to differ by district grade span.",
    "Centered student-teacher-ratio slope allowed to differ by district grade span."
  ),
  `Kept In Final Model` = ifelse(schools.engineered.pool %in% schools.final.terms, "Yes", "No"),
  check.names = FALSE
)

kable(schools.engineered.status, row.names = FALSE)
Term Type Meaning Kept In Final Model
income_c_sq Derived Squared centered district income. No
lunch_c_sq Derived Squared centered subsidized-lunch percentage. No
english_c_sq Derived Squared centered English-learner percentage. No
low_income_pressure_c Interaction Centered lunch percentage multiplied by centered English-learner percentage. Yes
income_lunch_c Interaction Centered income multiplied by centered lunch percentage. No
str_lunch_c Interaction Centered student-teacher ratio multiplied by centered lunch percentage. No
lunch_c:grades Interaction Centered lunch slope allowed to differ by district grade span. No
income_c:grades Interaction Centered income slope allowed to differ by district grade span. No
english_c:grades Interaction Centered English-learner slope allowed to differ by district grade span. No
str_c:grades Interaction Centered student-teacher-ratio slope allowed to differ by district grade span. No
  • CASchools is the stronger case for socioeconomic nonlinearities because poverty, income, and English-learner share overlap in the raw data.
  • Retained derived or interaction terms mean those patterns explain score variation beyond the original centered main effects.
  • Rejected engineered terms are still useful: they show AIC/CV did not need every possible curve or district-specific slope.

class: plot-slide small-table

CASchools: Diagnostics

Code 
# Visual residual checks for the final CASchools model.
par(mfrow = c(1, 2))
qqnorm(schools.model.final$residuals, main = "QQ Plot")
qqline(schools.model.final$residuals)
plot(
  schools.model.final$fitted.values,
  schools.model.final$residuals,
  pch = 16,
  xlab = "Fitted",
  ylab = "Residuals",
  main = "Residuals vs Fitted"
)
abline(h = 0, lty = "dashed")
Code 
par(mfrow = c(1, 1))
Code 
# Formal diagnostic p-values corresponding to the visual residual checks.
kable(schools.diag, row.names = FALSE)
Test P.Value
Shapiro-Wilk 0.37430
Breusch-Pagan 0.35933
Durbin-Watson 0.00814
  • The residual diagnostics are acceptable for this project; the larger interpretive question is coefficient meaning, not obvious model failure.

class: small-table plot-slide compact

CASchools: Box-Cox Response Check

Code 
# For CASchools, Box-Cox checks whether transforming the average score response
# materially improves assumptions. Lambda = 1 means keeping the original score
# scale.
schools.boxcox <- boxcox_fit_and_evaluate(
  schools.model.final,
  schools.train.new,
  schools.test,
  "score",
  "CASchools"
)

plot_boxcox_profile(schools.boxcox$profile, "CASchools Box-Cox profile")
Code 
# The decision is based on the profile interval plus whether original-scale
# held-out performance changes enough to justify harder interpretation.
kable(boxcox_decision_table(
  schools.boxcox,
  ifelse(
    schools.boxcox$profile$identity.in.interval,
    "Keep original score scale; lambda = 1 remains plausible.",
    "Use/report Box-Cox as sensitivity evidence; lambda = 1 is outside the profile interval."
  )
), row.names = FALSE)
Best Lambda Approx. 95% Lambda Interval Lambda 1 In Interval Decision
-0.32 [-2, 2] TRUE Keep original score scale; lambda = 1 remains plausible.
  • The profile peaks near lambda -0.32; lambda = 1 is inside the approximate 95% interval.
  • Because test score is naturally interpretable, Box-Cox would need clear diagnostic and prediction gains before replacing the original response.

class: small-table compact

CASchools: Box-Cox Test Metrics

Code 
# Compare original-scale held-out performance for the current final model and
# the Box-Cox alternatives.
kable(boxcox_performance_table(schools.boxcox), row.names = FALSE)
Dataset Model Lambda Test R2 Test RMSE Shapiro P BP P
CASchools Current final NA 0.8190 8.40 0.374 0.359
CASchools Box-Cox naive back-transform -0.32 0.8194 8.39 0.420 0.301
CASchools Box-Cox smearing back-transform -0.32 0.8190 8.40 0.420 0.301
  • RMSE is still measured in test-score points after back-transforming.
  • If the Box-Cox rows do not meaningfully improve held-out RMSE, the original score scale is easier to defend in presentation.
  • The diagnostic value is still useful because it shows we checked the response-transformation assumption rather than ignoring it.

class: plot-slide

CASchools: Bootstrap Coefficient Intervals

Code 
# Prepare the bootstrap coefficient interval plot for CASchools.
schools.boot.plot <- subset(schools.boot, term != "(Intercept)")
schools.boot.plot <- schools.boot.plot[nrow(schools.boot.plot):1, ]

plot(
  schools.boot.plot$estimate,
  seq_len(nrow(schools.boot.plot)),
  xlim = range(c(schools.boot.plot$lower, schools.boot.plot$upper)),
  pch = 16,
  yaxt = "n",
  ylab = "",
  xlab = "Coefficient estimate",
  main = "Bootstrap 95% intervals for final-model coefficients"
)

segments(
  schools.boot.plot$lower, seq_len(nrow(schools.boot.plot)),
  schools.boot.plot$upper, seq_len(nrow(schools.boot.plot)),
  lwd = 2,
  col = "#0b6e4f"
)

abline(v = 0, lty = "dashed", col = "gray50")
axis(2, at = seq_len(nrow(schools.boot.plot)), labels = schools.boot.plot$term, las = 2)
  • These are empirical 95% intervals from 200 bootstrap resamples.
  • A log scale would not work here because several coefficients are negative.
  • The intercept is omitted so the predictor intervals do not collapse visually toward 0.
  • lunch and income remain the most stable effects, while weaker terms sit closer to 0 and deserve more caution.

class: small-table

CASchools: Prediction Examples

Code 
# Show fitted values, confidence intervals, and prediction intervals for the
# two manually defined district examples.
kable(schools.pred.table, row.names = FALSE)
Scenario Fit CI PI
Lower-risk district 673.25 (670.69, 675.8) (659.15, 687.34)
Higher-risk district 638.21 (636.96, 639.45) (624.29, 652.12)
  • Lower lunch rates and higher district income lead to higher predicted scores in the final model.
  • The wider prediction intervals again show that district-level mean structure is estimated more tightly than individual-district outcome.

class: plot-slide

CASchools: CI and PI Overlay

Code 
# Overlay fitted means, CIs, and PIs on the cleaned CASchools training data.
plot(
  schools.fit.x,
  schools.train.new$score[schools.order],
  pch = 16,
  col = "gray40",
  xlab = "Fitted mean response (ordered low to high)",
  ylab = "Score",
  main = "Actual data with fitted CI/PI and new prediction intervals"
)

lines(schools.fit.x, schools.fit.x, col = "#0b6e4f", lwd = 2)
lines(schools.fit.x, schools.actual.conf[schools.order, "lwr"], col = "#0b6e4f", lty = 2)
lines(schools.fit.x, schools.actual.conf[schools.order, "upr"], col = "#0b6e4f", lty = 2)
lines(schools.fit.x, schools.actual.pred[schools.order, "lwr"], col = "#c97c10", lty = 3)
lines(schools.fit.x, schools.actual.pred[schools.order, "upr"], col = "#c97c10", lty = 3)

points(schools.new.fit, schools.new.fit, pch = 17, col = "#b83227", cex = 1.3)
segments(schools.new.fit, schools.pred[, "lwr"], schools.new.fit, schools.pred[, "upr"],
         col = "#f0b44c", lwd = 2)
segments(schools.new.fit, schools.conf[, "lwr"], schools.new.fit, schools.conf[, "upr"],
         col = "#b83227", lwd = 3)
  • The x-axis is fitted mean response, sorted from low to high.
  • New prediction cases now sit at their own fitted locations instead of being appended at the end.
  • The spread around the fitted line is consistent with the wider orange prediction bands for new districts.

class: plot-slide

CASchools: Predicted vs Actual (Test Set)

Code 
# Final visual check on the held-out CASchools test set.
plot(
  schools.prediction,
  schools.test$score,
  pch = 16,
  col = adjustcolor("#0b6e4f", alpha.f = 0.6),
  xlab = "Predicted Score",
  ylab = "Actual Score",
  main = "Predicted vs Actual — held-out test set"
)
abline(0, 1, col = "#b83227", lwd = 2, lty = 2)
legend(
  "topleft",
  legend = "Perfect prediction line (slope = 1)",
  col = "#b83227",
  lty = 2,
  lwd = 2,
  bty = "n"
)
  • The tight scatter around the diagonal shows the model generalizes well to unseen districts.
  • Residual spread is roughly even across score levels, consistent with the Breusch-Pagan result.
  • Any remaining deviations reflect unexplained district-level variation, not a systematic model failure.

class: center, middle, inverse

Part II

Finance Application

6 NYSE stocks, 3 years of close prices, and S&P 500 benchmarking

class: small-table compact

Finance: Method Steps

Code 
# Print the finance workflow table created in the setup chunk.
kable(finance.process, row.names = FALSE)
Step Detail
Pull close prices Yahoo Finance, 2023-2025
Convert to daily returns Arithmetic daily returns
Annualize return and risk Mean252 and sdsqrt(252)
Compare correlation to market Corr(stock, SP500)
Plot cumulative returns Growth of $1 over time
Run CAPM regressions alpha, beta, and R2
Track rolling beta 60-day beta windows
Track rolling correlation 60-day correlation windows
  • Returns, not raw prices, are the right object for comparison because the stocks trade at very different price levels.
  • The finance results answer different questions: average performance, market linkage, regression exposure, and time variation.

class: small-table

Finance: Stock Selection

Code 
# Display the six stock choices and their sectors.
kable(finance.stocks, row.names = FALSE)
Ticker Company Industry
JPM JPMorgan Chase Financial Services
JNJ Johnson & Johnson Healthcare
XOM Exxon Mobil Energy
WMT Walmart Retail & Wholesale
CAT Caterpillar Industrials
BA Boeing Company Aerospace
  • All six stocks are NYSE-listed.
  • Industries are intentionally different to avoid choosing six similar firms.
  • Benchmark: ^GSPC for the S&P 500.
  • The cross-sector mix gives the portfolio visibly different beta and correlation behavior instead of five nearly identical names.

class: plot-slide

Finance: Daily Return Distributions

Code 
# Compare daily return distributions for the six selected stocks.
par(mfrow = c(2, 3), mar = c(4, 3.5, 3, 1))

for (ticker in finance.stocks$Ticker) {
  hist(
    finance.ret.mat[, ticker],
    breaks = 40,
    col = "#2a6f97",
    border = "white",
    xlab = "Daily Return",
    main = ticker,
    cex.main = 1
  )
}
Code 
par(mfrow = c(1, 1), mar = c(5.1, 4.1, 4.1, 2.1))
  • All six stocks show fat-tailed return distributions — extreme days occur more often than a normal model predicts.
  • The spread of each histogram confirms the annualized risk ordering shown in the return-risk plot.
  • Comparing widths visually separates the more volatile names from the steadier defensive stocks.

class: plot-slide

Finance: Close Prices

Code 
# Plot raw closing prices for the six stocks only. SP500 is excluded here
# because it is a benchmark index, not a peer stock.
par(mfrow = c(3, 2))

plot(index(finance.close), as.numeric(finance.close[, "JPM"]),
     type = "l", xlab = "Date", ylab = "Close", main = "JPM")

plot(index(finance.close), as.numeric(finance.close[, "JNJ"]),
     type = "l", xlab = "Date", ylab = "Close", main = "JNJ")

plot(index(finance.close), as.numeric(finance.close[, "XOM"]),
     type = "l", xlab = "Date", ylab = "Close", main = "XOM")

plot(index(finance.close), as.numeric(finance.close[, "WMT"]),
     type = "l", xlab = "Date", ylab = "Close", main = "WMT")

plot(index(finance.close), as.numeric(finance.close[, "CAT"]),
     type = "l", xlab = "Date", ylab = "Close", main = "CAT")

plot(index(finance.close), as.numeric(finance.close[, "BA"]),
     type = "l", xlab = "Date", ylab = "Close", main = "BA")
Code 
par(mfrow = c(1, 1))
  • Raw close prices mainly show different scales and market regimes.
  • The next comparisons switch to returns because raw dollar levels are not directly comparable across stocks.

class: plot-slide

Finance: Annualized Return and Risk

Code 
# Risk-return scatter: x is annualized volatility, y is annualized mean return.
plot(
  finance.annual$Annual_Risk,
  finance.annual$Annual_Return,
  xlim = range(finance.annual$Annual_Risk) + c(-0.01, 0.02),
  ylim = range(finance.annual$Annual_Return) + c(-0.02, 0.03),
  pch = 21,
  bg = "#2a6f97",
  col = "#16384f",
  cex = 1.4,
  xlab = "Annualized Risk",
  ylab = "Annual Return",
  main = "Return-Risk"
)
text(
  finance.annual$Annual_Risk,
  finance.annual$Annual_Return,
  labels = finance.annual$Ticker,
  pos = finance.annual.label.pos,
  offset = 0.55,
  cex = 0.9,
  xpd = NA
)
  • CAT has the strongest annualized return, while BA carries the most risk.
  • JNJ is the defensive end of the group, so the chart separates aggressive from steadier names.

class: small-table plot-slide compact small-title

Finance: Correlation with S&P 500 vs Risk and Return

.pull-left[

Code 
# Table gives the numeric risk, return, and market-correlation values.
kable(finance.compare.display, row.names = FALSE)
Ticker Annual Return Annual Risk Corr with SP500
JPM 0.3198 0.2315 0.6023
JNJ 0.0650 0.1723 0.0811
XOM 0.0686 0.2270 0.2954
WMT 0.3049 0.1991 0.3494
CAT 0.3380 0.2899 0.6089
BA 0.0929 0.3333 0.4772

]

.pull-right[

Code 
# Bubble chart: position shows market correlation and return; bubble size
# represents annualized risk.
plot(
  finance.compare$Corr_SP500,
  finance.compare$Annual_Return,
  type = "n",
  xlim = range(finance.compare$Corr_SP500) + c(-0.08, 0.08),
  ylim = range(finance.compare$Annual_Return) + c(-0.03, 0.05),
  xlab = "Corr with SP500",
  ylab = "Annual Return",
  main = "Market correlation vs return"
)

symbols(
  finance.compare$Corr_SP500,
  finance.compare$Annual_Return,
  circles = finance.compare$Annual_Risk,
  inches = 0.2,
  add = TRUE,
  bg = adjustcolor("#2a6f97", alpha.f = 0.25),
  fg = "#2a6f97"
)

text(
  finance.compare$Corr_SP500,
  finance.compare$Annual_Return,
  labels = finance.compare$Ticker,
  pos = finance.label.pos,
  offset = 0.8,
  cex = 0.8,
  xpd = NA
)

]

  • Horizontal position is correlation with the S&P 500, vertical position is annualized return, and bubble size reflects annualized risk.
  • CAT is the most market-linked stock here, but that does not automatically make it the highest-return name.
  • JNJ helps show that lower market linkage can pair with a very different return-risk profile.

class: plot-slide

Finance: Linking Risk-Return and Market Correlation

Code 
# This custom plot puts two views on the same y-axis: risk-return on the left
# and market-correlation-return on the right. Dotted segments link each stock.
risk.x <- 0.8 + (finance.compare$Annual_Risk - min(finance.compare$Annual_Risk)) /
  diff(range(finance.compare$Annual_Risk)) * 1.0
corr.x <- 3.2 + (finance.compare$Corr_SP500 - min(finance.compare$Corr_SP500)) /
  diff(range(finance.compare$Corr_SP500)) * 1.0
ylims <- range(finance.compare$Annual_Return) + c(-0.03, 0.05)

plot(
  NA,
  xlim = c(0.4, 4.6),
  ylim = ylims,
  xaxt = "n",
  xlab = "",
  ylab = "Annual Return",
  main = "Risk-return points linked to market-correlation bubbles"
)

rect(0.55, ylims[1], 2.0, ylims[2], border = "#d9cdb7", col = adjustcolor("#f7efe1", alpha.f = 0.55))
rect(3.0, ylims[1], 4.45, ylims[2], border = "#d9cdb7", col = adjustcolor("#eef6f4", alpha.f = 0.65))
axis(1, at = c(1.275, 3.6), labels = c("Annualized Risk", "Corr with SP500"))

for (i in 1:nrow(finance.compare)) {
  segments(risk.x[i], finance.compare$Annual_Return[i], corr.x[i], finance.compare$Annual_Return[i],
           lty = 3, lwd = 1.5, col = adjustcolor("#7a8793", alpha.f = 0.8))
}

points(risk.x, finance.compare$Annual_Return, pch = 16, col = "#16384f", cex = 1.2)
symbols(
  corr.x,
  finance.compare$Annual_Return,
  circles = finance.compare$Annual_Risk,
  inches = 0.12,
  add = TRUE,
  bg = adjustcolor("#2a6f97", alpha.f = 0.25),
  fg = "#2a6f97"
)

text(risk.x, finance.compare$Annual_Return, labels = finance.compare$Ticker, pos = 2, offset = 0.6, cex = 0.8)
text(corr.x, finance.compare$Annual_Return, labels = finance.compare$Ticker, pos = 4, offset = 0.6, cex = 0.8)
  • Dotted links connect each stock’s annual return across the risk view and the market-correlation view.
  • This makes it easier to compare whether a stock’s return profile is lining up more with risk or with broad-market linkage.
  • A stock can be very market-linked without having the most extreme risk position, so correlation and volatility are related but not the same.

class: plot-slide

Finance: Correlation Heatmap

Code 
# Heatmap version of the return-correlation matrix; warmer colors mean stronger
# positive return co-movement.
par(mar = c(6, 6, 4, 2))

image(
  1:ncol(finance.corr),
  1:nrow(finance.corr),
  t(finance.corr[nrow(finance.corr):1, ]),
  col = colorRampPalette(c("#16384f", "#fbf7ef", "#b84d32"))(100),
  zlim = c(-1, 1),
  axes = FALSE,
  xlab = "",
  ylab = "",
  main = "Return correlation heatmap"
)

axis(1, at = 1:ncol(finance.corr), labels = colnames(finance.corr), las = 2)
axis(2, at = 1:nrow(finance.corr), labels = rev(rownames(finance.corr)), las = 2)

for (i in 1:nrow(finance.corr)) {
  for (j in 1:ncol(finance.corr)) {
    text(j, nrow(finance.corr) - i + 1, labels = sprintf("%.2f", finance.corr[i, j]), cex = 0.8)
  }
}
Code 
par(mar = c(5.1, 4.1, 4.1, 2.1))
  • Warmer colors mean stronger positive correlation.
  • Cooler colors mean weaker market co-movement.
  • Correlation matters because diversification is weaker when all assets move together.
  • The uneven color pattern shows that diversification benefit depends on which names are combined, not just how many names are held.

class: plot-slide

Finance: Cumulative Returns

Code 
# Convert daily returns into a growth-of-one-dollar path for each stock and
# the SP500 benchmark.
matplot(
  index(finance.cum),
  coredata(finance.cum),
  type = "l",
  lty = 1,
  lwd = 2,
  col = 1:7,
  xlab = "Date",
  ylab = "Growth of $1",
  main = "Cumulative Returns"
)

legend(
  "topleft",
  legend = colnames(finance.cum),
  col = 1:7,
  lty = 1,
  lwd = 2,
  bty = "n"
)
  • CAT finishes with the strongest growth path, while BA trails the group.
  • The path differences show why average return alone can hide very different investment experiences.

class: small-table

Finance: Alpha, Beta, and R-Squared

Code 
# CAPM summary table from six separate regressions of stock returns on SP500
# returns.
kable(finance.capm.display, row.names = FALSE)
Ticker Alpha Daily Alpha Annual Beta R Squared
JPM 5e-04 0.1261 0.9257 0.3628
JNJ 2e-04 0.0456 0.0927 0.0066
XOM -1e-04 -0.0246 0.4452 0.0873
WMT 8e-04 0.2083 0.4618 0.1221
CAT 4e-04 0.0929 1.1718 0.3707
BA -5e-04 -0.1280 1.0558 0.2277
  • beta measures sensitivity to market return.
  • alpha measures abnormal return relative to the S&P 500.
  • R^2 measures how much of stock-return variation is explained by the market.
  • CAT is the most market-sensitive name here, and CAT is the stock whose returns are most tightly tied to broad market movement.

class: plot-slide

Finance: CAPM Fitted vs Actual Returns

Code 
# For each stock, re-fit the CAPM regression and compare actual daily returns
# against the fitted daily returns from the market factor.
par(mfrow = c(2, 3), mar = c(4, 3.5, 3, 1))

for (ticker in finance.stocks$Ticker) {
  model <- lm(finance.ret.mat[, ticker] ~ finance.ret.mat[, "SP500"])
  plot(
    fitted(model),
    finance.ret.mat[, ticker],
    pch = 16,
    col = adjustcolor("#2a6f97", alpha.f = 0.35),
    cex = 0.6,
    xlab = "CAPM Fitted",
    ylab = "Actual Return",
    main = ticker,
    cex.main = 1
  )
  abline(0, 1, col = "#b83227", lwd = 1.5, lty = 2)
}
Code 
par(mfrow = c(1, 1), mar = c(5.1, 4.1, 4.1, 2.1))
  • Stocks with higher R-squared cluster more tightly around the diagonal; lower R-squared stocks show wider scatter.
  • The spread in each panel is the stock-specific variation the CAPM market factor cannot explain.
  • This complements the R-squared column in the CAPM table by making the unexplained residual variation visible.

class: plot-slide

Finance: Rolling 60-Day Beta

Code 
# Plot the rolling market beta created in the setup chunk. Each point uses the
# prior 60 trading days, so changes show time-varying market exposure.
par(mar = c(7, 4.1, 4.1, 2.1))

matplot(
  index(finance.roll.beta),
  coredata(finance.roll.beta),
  type = "l",
  lty = 1,
  lwd = 2,
  col = 1:6,
  xlab = "Date",
  ylab = "Rolling 60-day beta",
  main = "Rolling beta relative to the S&P 500"
)

legend(
  "bottom",
  inset = c(0, -0.28),
  legend = colnames(finance.roll.beta),
  col = 1:6,
  lty = 1,
  lwd = 2,
  horiz = TRUE,
  xpd = NA,
  bty = "n"
)
Code 
par(mar = c(5.1, 4.1, 4.1, 2.1))
  • Beta above 1 means the stock moved more aggressively than the market in that 60-day window.
  • Beta blends co-movement with relative volatility, so a rising line can mean tighter market linkage, larger stock swings, or both.
  • A single full-sample beta misses these shifts, so rolling beta is more informative about changing exposure.

class: plot-slide

Finance: Rolling 60-Day Correlation

Code 
# Plot the rolling 60-day correlation with SP500. This isolates co-movement
# from volatility, unlike beta.
par(mar = c(7, 4.1, 4.1, 2.1))

matplot(
  index(finance.roll.corr),
  coredata(finance.roll.corr),
  type = "l",
  lty = 1,
  lwd = 2,
  col = 1:6,
  xlab = "Date",
  ylab = "Rolling 60-day correlation",
  main = "Rolling correlation with the S&P 500"
)

legend(
  "bottom",
  inset = c(0, -0.28),
  legend = colnames(finance.roll.corr),
  col = 1:6,
  lty = 1,
  lwd = 2,
  horiz = TRUE,
  xpd = NA,
  bty = "n"
)
Code 
par(mar = c(5.1, 4.1, 4.1, 2.1))
  • Correlation isolates co-movement strength only, without the relative-volatility piece.
  • Together, the rolling beta and rolling correlation plots show whether a stock became more market-linked because it moved more in sync with the S&P 500, because it got more volatile, or both.
  • Comparing the two rolling plots separates “moves with the market” from “moves more violently than the market.”

class: center, middle, inverse

Final Takeaways

  • HousePrices: centered derived and interaction candidates enter before AIC/CV; the final post-Cook model uses Step 6 and keeps log_lotsize_c as derived terms plus garage_c:lotsize_1000_c as interactions.
  • HousePrices: VIF is documented but not used as a pre-filter, and Box-Cox is treated as sensitivity evidence for a transformed response.
  • CASchools: the final post-Cook model uses Step 12, keeps None as derived terms and low_income_pressure_c as interactions, and still points primarily to poverty, income, and district type.
  • CASchools: Box-Cox is not strong enough to replace the original score scale.
  • Finance: the six stocks occupy different risk-return positions, show different market linkage to the S&P 500, and that linkage changes over time in the rolling beta and rolling correlation plots.
